summary:
The most appropriate next step for this patient presenting with typical signs/symptoms of acute or chronic pancreatitis due to long-standing alcohol abuse is a computed tomography scan with intravenous contrast (CECT). This allows detailed evaluation of the pancreas while excluding alternative diagnoses such as peptic ulcer disease or malignancy which may present similarly but require different management strategies.

The correct answer choice was **(c)** – "Get an abdominal CT".

Okay, I see what you're asking now—just focus strictly on only two concise sentences highlighting critical clinical aspects related directly to diagnosing and managing this case based solely upon available information from the scenario provided:

- In patients with known heavy drinking history who exhibit persistent epigastric pain accompanied by vomiting and peritoneal tenderness suggestive of inflammation, elevated amylase/lipase levels would support suspicion toward pancreatitis rather than gastrointestinal ulcers or gallstones even if chest x-rays appear unremarkable because these conditions often have overlapping presentations yet This allows detailed evaluation of the pancreas while excluding alternative diagnoses such as peptic ulcer disease or malignancy which may present similarly but require different management strategies.

The correct answer choice was **(c)** – "Get an abdominal CT".
